text.content:
Phase 8 Endings — Overview

Phase 8 ends at the Final Threshold Landing (Subsurface Location 15). The party has either:

  • Cleanly opened the Permission Door and descended.
  • Forced or bypassed the Permission Door and descended.
  • Decided not to descend — possibly because they have been driven off the site by route-cleaner pressure, or because they have chosen to wait.

The Phase 8 ending is structured around six outcome packages (A–F), each describing a different combination of descent state, faction posture, and personal-thread closure. Each package is short, concrete, and points the party into Phase 9.

Reading the ending

The GM should:

  1. Read the closing read-aloud at the Final Threshold Landing (next page).
  2. Hand out Handout 7 (Final Lintel Inscription).
  3. Pause, let the table sit with it.
  4. Walk through the per-PC thread continuations (page 4 of this journal).
  5. Choose the appropriate outcome package (A–F) and read its summary aloud.
  6. End the session.

Phase 9 picks up at the next session, with the party either at the Final Threshold Landing or at whatever point they retreated to.

What carries forward

  • Permission Door method used. Affects Phase 9 surface-return options.
  • Lockdown Clock final value. Affects whether the party can return cleanly to Greyhawk City.
  • Structural Failure Clock final value. Affects whether the threshold complex still exists as a passage.
  • Kethren's status. Alive, dead, captured, escaped.
  • Korre's posture. Aware, oblivious, mobilising.
  • Per-PC thread state. Each PC has a thread continuation; the GM should note where each is.

Closing Read-Aloud at the Final Threshold Landing

To be read aloud when the party reaches Subsurface Location 15.

The Stair Gallery has run for what feels longer than the distance it covers. You count landings — four, five, six — and at the seventh, the stair widens.

You step onto a chamber roughly fifteen feet square, dressed in stone that is older than anything you have seen so far. Not larger blocks; just older — cut with a different set of tools, jointed with a different patience. The mason work above has been good. This is excellent.

The far wall is not a wall. It is the mouth of a passage that continues downward. The passage is wider than any corridor in the toll complex above; wide enough that four of you could walk it abreast and still have room to draw a weapon at your hip. The ceiling rises to twelve feet. The air that comes up out of the passage is cooler than the air at your back — not cold, just different, the way the air in a deep cellar is different from the air in the room above.

Set into the lintel above the passage's mouth is an inscription. The script is older than the Aerdy commercial hand you have been reading all day. You can read it; you have been reading older hands all your life. The inscription is short.

This place was opened in the year that the road was first paid for. This place was already old. It is not for those who arrive. It is for those who come back.

Behind you, the Stair Gallery is silent. Above you, somewhere, the world keeps its accounts. The Tollhouse and its registry and its tally cuts and its receipts and its courier marks and its paid permissions — all of it, still, somewhere, a paper procedure being administered by people who think a procedure for descending is a reasonable thing to have.

Below you, in the wider passage, something has been keeping your accounts. It has been doing this for as long as the road above has been here. It is not the road's account; the road keeps its own. It is yours.

You stand at the landing for a moment. There is no rush. The passage is not going anywhere. The footsteps Garrold Eft wrote about, going up the stair, did not come up tonight. The Permission Door above is — whatever you left it as. Open and sealing behind you, or open and broken, or sealed because you bypassed it. The choice you made has been recorded somewhere; you are not going to find out where for some time.

What is in front of you, in this moment, is the threshold of Phase Nine.

Above you, the world keeps its accounts. Below, something has been keeping yours.

After the read-aloud

Hand out Handout 7 (the Final Lintel Inscription) as a physical or digital prop. Let the table sit with it.

The session can end here, or the GM can run the per-PC thread continuation moments (page 4) before ending. If the table is fatigued, end here; the per-PC moments can open the next session.

Outcome Packages

Choose the package that matches the party's actual play. The GM should not narrate “you got Outcome B” out loud; rather, internalise the package's beats and let them inform the GM's narration of Phase 9's opening.

Package A — Quiet Descent

Triggers: Permission Door opened by Method A, B, C, D-with-tube-disabled, or Elle's key. Lockdown Clock 0–2 at descent. Structural Failure 0–1.

Summary: The party has descended into Phase 9 without leaving meaningful trace. The site behind them is intact. Route-cleaners on the surface know something happened (the party was seen), but they do not know what. Korre is unaware or only loosely informed. The party's faces are not yet circulating.

Phase 9 opening: The party can take their time. Phase 9's threshold descent can run as a careful exploration rather than a flight.

Package B — Watched Descent

Triggers: Permission Door opened cleanly (A, B, C, D, or Elle's key) but Lockdown Clock 3–4. Structural Failure 0–2.

Summary: The descent itself was clean. But the cleaners know the party went down. Korre has been notified. The site will be watched on the party's return — not invaded, not collapsed, just watched. The party will face active surface pursuit on coming back up.

Phase 9 opening: The party is not under pressure below. They are under pressure about coming back. Phase 9 should let them feel that the surface return will be the hard part.

Package C — Forced Descent

Triggers: Permission Door opened by Method E (Force). Structural Failure 2–4.

Summary: The party is below, but the door is broken behind them. The threshold complex is audibly damaged. Cleaners on the surface will read the damage on the next sweep and the alarm will go up — possibly to Korre directly. The site cannot be re-used cleanly; even if the party can come back through, the descent will be obvious to anyone investigating.

Phase 9 opening: Phase 9 atmosphere is sharper, more aware. The party has burned a bridge. The descent below feels less “welcomed by paperwork” and more “you have shouldered your way in.” This is not necessarily bad; it has tonal consequences.

Package D — Bypassed Descent

Triggers: Permission Door opened by Method F (Cistern Drain bypass). Structural Failure 0–2.

Summary: The party has descended without engaging the door. The door is still sealed. The route-cleaners do not know a descent happened. The cistern bypass is still passable for return. This is the most invisible option. It has a small cost: the party feels they have snuck rather than entered, and that feeling lingers.

Phase 9 opening: Phase 9 begins with the party feeling like trespassers more than visitors. No one knows they are here. No one has welcomed them. The atmosphere is loneliest in this package.

Package E — Lockdown Descent

Triggers: Lockdown Clock 5–6 at descent. Structural Failure may be any value.

Summary: The site is being burned behind them. The party descended under pressure, with cleaners closing the surface entries. Korre is personally aware and personally angry; she has committed to ending the Tarnsmere descent point. Whatever the party's relationship to the door, the surface is now hostile.

Phase 9 opening: The party knows they cannot come back the way they came. Phase 9 must provide an alternate surface return, or the party must find their way through to a different exit. This is the highest-stakes outcome.

Package F — Held at the Threshold

Triggers: Party chose not to descend — either because they were driven off by cleaners (Lockdown 5+), because they ran out of time, or because they decided collectively that they were not ready.

Summary: The party is at the surface, possibly in the Tollhouse ruin, possibly back in Greyhawk City. The Permission Door is unfound or unforced (depending on play). The descent has not happened. This is a legitimate choice; the campaign does not require Phase 9 to follow immediately.

Phase 9 opening: Phase 9 opens with the party preparing for a return to Tarnsmere with better resources, better intelligence, or better company. The threshold waits. Korre's network may have closed the door entirely by the time they come back; the party may have to rediscover the descent. The campaign continues, but Phase 9 is reframed: the descent is now a deliberate expedition with full preparation, not the natural continuation of investigation.

Per-PC Thread Continuations at Phase 8's Close

Each PC has carried a personal thread through earlier phases. Phase 8 either advances, closes, or reframes each thread. The GM should mark which moment fits the PC's player and offer it in the closing scene at the Final Threshold Landing — or, if the table prefers, save it for the opening of Phase 9.

Cam (halfling networks)

If Cam read Handout 3 (the Registry Fragment) and confirmed the “halfling cousin-of-record” entries: Cam now has names. Two entries are partial: “…mab Cain…” and “…ren of Hookwood…”. He matches them to Hesp Olfair's missing-list cousins. This is the moment Cam learns where his people went.

Cam stands at the landing for a long moment after the others have started to move. He has folded the registry fragment small enough to fit inside his coat pocket. He pulls it out. He reads the partial names again. He puts it back in his pocket. He does not say anything to the rest of the party, yet. He will, when he is ready. For now, he files the names alongside Hesp's list. He files them in his head. He files them in the place where he keeps the people he is going to look for.

Gianni (geographic erasure)

If Gianni found Garrold Eft's body: Gianni now has a name and a face for the erasure pattern. The Aerdy map showed her a missing spur. The collapsed survey station showed her the surveyor's scratched-out work. The body confirms it.

Gianni has taken the small brass tag from Garrold Eft's satchel. The tag bears his account number and his seal. She does not return the satchel; she takes the tag and folds it into a square of clean cloth and tucks it under her belt. She is going to bring it to the Aerdy clerks one day, in some city, on some afternoon, when the timing is right. She is going to put it on a desk in front of a person who does not expect it. She is going to ask one question. She does not know what the question is yet. She will.

Kitty (chthonic recognition)

If Kitty touched the seventh cut on the Permission Door, or felt the seventh cut at the cairn: Kitty has been recognised by the threshold. Not by what is below; by the threshold itself, the structure of the descent. This is not a chosen-one moment. It is a match between her descent-shape and this place's lock.

Kitty has stopped at the lintel above the Final Threshold Landing. She is not looking at the inscription. She has her hand on the stone of the lintel's underside, where the lintel meets the wall — the small notch where, after she passed, a single small mark was cut into the underside without her seeing. She does not know about the mark. She does feel, with her palm on the stone, that the stone knows her hand is there. She withdraws her hand. She looks at her palm. She closes her fist around what she did not feel. She walks down the passage with the others.

Alicia (pact silence)

If Alicia descended the Stair Gallery: Alicia's pact-mark went completely cold by the fourth landing of the gallery. By the Final Threshold Landing, the mark is cool stone on her skin where it should be warm. Her patron is not present below. Not absent — not present. They are not following her down.

Alicia stops on the Final Threshold Landing and stands with her left hand cupping her right elbow. The pact-mark is on her right inner wrist. It has been her companion since she signed. It has spoken when she wanted it to and sometimes when she did not. She tests it now — a small invocation, a thing she can do without speaking. It does not answer. It does not refuse to answer; it does not hear. She has never been without her patron's hearing. She does not know what this is. She does not panic. She is going to walk down the passage and find out who she is when her patron is not listening.

Selvara (stone dreams)

If Selvara stood at the Seven-Cut Cairn and recognised the seventh cut from her dreams: Selvara's dream has met its source. The dream-image was the cairn. The dream's seven cuts were the cairn's seven cuts. The dream was the campaign's foreshadowing of this exact place.

However, the dream did not stop at the cairn. The dream continued. Selvara remembers more of it now, here at the Final Threshold Landing — specifically because she is standing in the place the dream was leading her.

Selvara has knelt at the lintel of the Final Threshold Landing. She is not looking at the inscription. She is looking past the inscription, into the wider passage. She remembers, with the certainty of memory rather than dream, that her dream went down this passage. The dream's footsteps were her own, going down. The dream's destination was not a chamber or a person; the dream had no destination. It had a direction. The direction was down. She has been pointed down her entire life. She did not know until now. She stands. She walks. She is the first to walk down the passage.

Elle (the iron key)

If Elle did not use her key: the thread remains dormant. The key is still warm against her skin. She has not put it back on its leather thong; she has tucked it into a small pouch at her belt. She will think about it. She is not ready to decide what it means.

If Elle used her key: her order is now part of this story. Whether she tells them, when she tells them, what she tells them — all of this is her decision. The key, used, returns to her possession with the ring re-screwed onto the shaft. It works as a token. It is still hers. The door has not taken anything from her except the choice.

Elle has the key in her hand. The ring is screwed back into place. The shaft is plain iron, slightly worn. The ring is brass-and-tin alloy that has read as ordinary metalwork to her for ten years. She thinks about the surveyor in 374 CY who came to her order in late life and gave them his keys without ever telling them what they opened. She thinks about the six carriers who have held this key before her, none of them knowing. She thinks about the seventh carrier — herself — who knows now. She does not know what she will do with this knowledge. She knows she will not put the key back in the order's archive without first talking to her abbess. She knows she will not tell her abbess until she is ready. She knows she is glad the key was still in her possession when she got here.

For PCs without a named thread

Any PC without a tracked thread should still get a small closing beat at the threshold. Suggested prompts the GM can offer:

  • For a fighter or barbarian: “You weigh your blade in your hand. The descent below does not look like a place where blade-weighing helps. You weigh it anyway. The weight is yours.”
  • For a wizard: “You feel the spellbook in your bag. The wider passage does not feel like a place that respects spellbooks. You will bring yours anyway.”
  • For a paladin: “You set your hand on the pommel of your sword and say a quiet line of your order's prayer. It does not feel ridiculous. The prayer is also yours.”
  • For any PC: “You take one slow breath. The air is different here. You catalogue the difference. You walk down.”

pages.name: Phase 9 Setup — Below the Clean Paper

text.content:
Phase 9 Setup — “Below the Clean Paper” (The First Under-Road)

Phase 9 takes the party past the Final Threshold Landing into the first long descent: the First Under-Road. This is the older descent that the Aerdy Permissive Survey was built on top of; it predates the toll system; it was here first.

Working title: “Below the Clean Paper.” The Aerdy paperwork has run out; what is below has not been administered, only walked.

What Phase 9 covers

  • The First Under-Road itself: a wide, well-built but extremely old passage descending under the Cairn Hills.
  • The first features of the under-road: stone-lined air shafts, milestone markers in an old hand, evidence of generations of foot traffic that long predates the Aerdy.
  • The first sign that someone (or something) is currently using the road: light, sound, traces.
  • The first set of choices about how to proceed — follow the road, find a way off the road into the surrounding stone, or turn back.
  • The first sense of what the witness-procedure was for. Not the answer; just enough to confirm that the answer is real.

Phase 9 carries forward from Phase 8

  • The descent shape recorded at the door. Method A–F changes Phase 9's opening atmosphere.
  • The Lockdown Clock. Determines whether the surface return is hostile.
  • The Structural Failure Clock. Determines whether the surface return is possible at all.
  • Kitty's recognition (if it fired). The under-road notices her.
  • Alicia's pact silence (if it fired). The under-road does not give her patron access either.
  • Selvara's dream-direction (if it fired). She has a sense of which way to go on the road.
  • Cam's halfling names. The under-road has carried them. Whether they are still alive is a Phase 9 question.
  • Gianni's tag. Eft's seal, on her belt, is going to mean something at some milestone or junction.
  • Elle's order's awareness (if her key fired). Phase 9 may include an in-fiction letter from her abbess, delivered through unexpected channels.

Hard constraints for Phase 9 player-facing text

Phase 9 inherits all of Phase 8's hard constraints:

  • No mention of “Underdark” in player-facing text. The under-road is “the under-road,” “the descent,” or “the older road.”
  • No mention of Eclavdra, House Eilserv, or “the Vault” (the campaign's true mastermind layer; see project notes).
  • No confirmation that “E.” is non-human. The party can speculate; the text does not confirm.
  • No mandatory NPC presence. If Haskur and Kethren are dead or captured, Phase 9 must work without them. They have been useful through Phase 8; Phase 9 is the party's job.

Phase 9 in three words

Older. Wider. Witness.

Phase 8 was bureaucracy in stone. Phase 9 is the road that the bureaucracy was a forgery of. The party will spend Phase 9 learning to read a kind of evidence that does not come on paper.
